items.correct: A book-length study of a single society based on fieldwork.
items.misconception: A short statistical survey of many societies.
items.why: Students confuse the in-depth single-society focus of a monograph with broad survey research.

items.q: What does 'thick description' add to a plain account of behaviour?
items.correct: The layered cultural meanings that make the behaviour intelligible.
items.misconception: Nothing; it just repeats the behaviour twice.
items.random_wrong: A numerical score for each action.
items.why: Learners miss that thick description interprets meaning rather than simply restating actions.

items.q: How should an ethnography be read 'holistically'?
items.correct: By attending to how its elements, such as ritual and economy, connect as a whole.
items.misconception: By reading only the chapter that seems most interesting.
items.random_wrong: By focusing solely on the bibliography.
items.why: Students treat parts of a culture in isolation, missing the holistic connections anthropologists stress.

items.q: A book-length ethnographic study of a single society is called a:
items.why: It's Monograph. “Questionnaire” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: 'Thick description', recording layered cultural meaning, is a term coined by:
items.why: Correct answer: Clifford Geertz. “Charles Darwin” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A community member who provides information to the anthropologist is an:
items.why: Correct answer: Informant. “Examiner” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Reading an ethnography 'holistically' means attending to:
items.correct: How its elements connect as a whole
items.why: Correct answer: How its elements connect as a whole. “Only the introduction” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
